preamble: Spirantization of [pʰ] to [ɸ] involves the change of a voiceless aspirated bilabial stop into a voiceless bilabial fricative. This is a common form of lenition where the closure of the stop is incomplete.

examples.note: In the transition from Ancient to Modern Greek, the aspirated stops became fricatives. [pʰ] shifted to [ɸ] before eventually becoming labiodental [f].

Grimm's Law in Germanic involved the shift of voiceless stops to fricatives. While PIE *p was not aspirated, the process [p] → [pʰ] → [ɸ] → [f] is the standard reconstruction for the development of English 'f' (e.g., 'father' vs Latin 'pater').

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
